2606.05720.title: Microskill Architecture: A Modular Skill-Driven Framework for AI-Native Code Generation
2606.05720.paper_summary: Large language models and AI coding agents have reshaped software development, but the path to fully AI-native systems faces structural challenges. Chief among them is managing context windows without losing accuracy or efficiency. When developers inject full project documentation and code into a model's memory, the model loses mid-sequence information, token costs spiral, and architecture drifts. This paper presents MicroSkill Architecture: a modular design paradigm inspired by microservices, applied to knowledge encapsulation instead of service decomposition. Instead of feeding an agent the entire codebase, the architecture partitions knowledge into atomic, sharply scoped skill capsules, and a dynamic router selects only semantically relevant capsules for the task. We formally model context allocation as constrained optimization over semantic relevance subject to a token budget. An empirical case study an enterprise content management system with fifteen complex features shows that MicroSkill cuts token consumption by over 90%, nearly doubles first-try compilation success rates, eliminates architectural violations entirely, and enables autonomous extraction and registration of seven new skill capsules via a self-learning mechanism. These findings suggest MicroSkill Architecture offers a scalable foundation for building AI-native development systems that are more efficient, more reliable, and capable of evolving over time.
2606.05720.paper_summary_zh: 大型語言模型和人工智慧編碼代理已經重塑了軟體開發，但完全原生於人工智慧系統的道路面臨結構性挑戰。最主要的挑戰是如何在不失去準確性或效率的情況下管理上下文窗口。當開發者將完整的專案文檔和代碼注入模型的記憶中時，模型會失去中序列的信息，令標記成本飆升，架構也會漂移。本文提出了微技能架構：一種受到微服務啟發的模組化設計範式，應用於知識封裝而非服務拆解。這種架構並不是將整個代碼庫提供給代理，而是將知識劃分為原子化、範圍明確的技能膠囊，並由動態路由器選擇僅與任務語義相關的膠囊。我們正式將上下文分配建模為在標記預算限制下的語義相關性約束優化。對於一個擁有十五個複雜功能的企業內容管理系統的實證案例研究顯示，微技能架構將標記消耗減少了超過90%，幾乎將第一次編譯成功率翻倍，完全消除了架構違規，並通過自學機制實現了七個新技能膠囊的自主提取和註冊。這些發現表明，微技能架構為構建更高效、更可靠且能隨時間演變的人工智慧原生開發系統提供了可擴展的基礎。
2606.05720.author: Mohammad Zare et.al.
